title: Stateful AI Agents with LangGraph

modules.title: Beyond Chains: Why Stateful Agents Require a Graph-Based Execution Model
modules.sub: Understanding the limits of linear chains for complex agentic tasks
modules.description: Learners examine where sequential chain-based pipelines break down when agents must branch, retry, or resume across multiple steps. They analyze concrete failure scenarios — lost context, unrecoverable errors, no decision points — that motivate the shift to a graph-based execution model. By the end, they can articulate precisely why stateful, cyclical execution is a prerequisite for reliable agentic systems.

modules.title: LangGraph Core Concepts: Nodes, Edges, State Schema, and the Execution Loop
modules.sub: Building fluency with LangGraph's fundamental structural and runtime primitives
modules.description: Learners map the core vocabulary of LangGraph — nodes as discrete execution units, edges as conditional routing logic, and state schema as the shared memory object passed through the graph. They trace how the execution loop processes a graph step by step, observing how state is read, mutated, and forwarded. Hands-on exercises have learners construct a minimal working graph to cement each concept before moving to more complex designs.

modules.title: Designing Agent Decision Loops: When to Act, When to Pause, When to Retry
modules.sub: Encoding conditional logic so agents behave reliably under uncertainty
modules.description: Learners design the core decision logic that governs an agent's behavior at each step — choosing between taking an action, waiting for new input, or retrying after a failure. They implement conditional edges that route execution based on state values, tool outcomes, and error signals. The module focuses on making this logic explicit and testable rather than burying it inside model prompts.

modules.title: Multi-Agent Architectures: Orchestrators, Subagents, and Handoff Protocols
modules.sub: Structuring multiple cooperating agents with clear roles and boundaries
modules.description: Learners design systems where a coordinating orchestrator agent delegates specialized subtasks to purpose-built subagents, then receives and integrates their results. They define handoff protocols — what state is passed, in what format, and under what conditions — to prevent information loss and coordination failures. By the end, learners can sketch and partially implement a multi-agent topology suited to a decomposable real-world task.

modules.title: Persistence and Memory: Checkpointing State Across Sessions and Interruptions
modules.sub: Ensuring agents can pause, resume, and recover without losing critical context
modules.description: Learners configure LangGraph's checkpointing mechanisms to serialize agent state at defined points so that workflows can survive interruptions, crashes, or intentional pauses. They evaluate different persistence backends and choose appropriate checkpointing granularity for their use case. The module also covers how to reload a prior checkpoint and resume execution cleanly, a critical capability for long-running production workflows.

modules.title: Human-in-the-Loop Control: Interrupts, Approvals, and Override Mechanisms
modules.sub: Inserting human judgment at high-stakes decision points without breaking flow
modules.description: Learners implement interrupt nodes that pause agent execution and surface a decision or artifact for human review before proceeding. They design approval workflows in which the agent waits for an explicit human signal — approve, reject, or redirect — and routes accordingly. The module addresses both the technical implementation and the design question of where human oversight adds the most value versus slowing a workflow unnecessarily.

modules.title: Apply It: Design a Stateful Agent for a Multi-Step Task in Your Domain
modules.sub: Translating course concepts into a concrete agent design for your own work
modules.description: Learners select a multi-step task from their own professional domain and produce a complete LangGraph agent design: a state schema, a node-and-edge graph diagram, decision logic, checkpointing strategy, and at least one human-in-the-loop control point. They document the design rationale — why each structural choice was made — to demonstrate understanding rather than just reproduction. This module serves as both a capstone practice exercise and a reusable blueprint they can carry into implementation.

modules.title: Testing, Observability, and Failure Modes in Production Agent Systems
modules.sub: Making stateful agents debuggable, auditable, and resilient under real conditions
modules.description: Learners apply testing strategies specific to graph-based agents — unit-testing individual nodes, simulating edge conditions, and replaying checkpointed states to reproduce failures. They instrument their graphs with observability tooling to trace execution paths, inspect state at each step, and surface unexpected branches. The module closes with a structured review of common production failure modes — infinite loops, state corruption, tool timeouts — and the design patterns that prevent or contain them.

synopsis: LangGraph extends the LangChain ecosystem to handle agents that must maintain state, branch on decisions, and recover from failures across multi-step tasks — capabilities that simple chains cannot provide. This course equips professionals to architect reliable, controllable agentic systems rather than brittle one-shot pipelines.
rationale: As AI agents move from demos to production, stateful orchestration frameworks like LangGraph are becoming essential knowledge for anyone building or overseeing systems that must handle complex, multi-step reasoning with real-world consequences.
learning_outcome: Professionals can design and implement a stateful LangGraph agent with defined decision logic, human override points, and checkpointed state management suitable for a real multi-step workflow in their domain.
